LLM Inference Overview Architecture Methodology Baseline Attention KV-Cache Combined Quality Q&A

Efficient Transformer Inference on Commodity Hardware

Optimizing Attention and KV-Cache Under 4 GB VRAM Constraints

Jay Darji Karma Patel San Jose State University RTX 3050 Ti • 4 GB VRAM

🏆 Key Findings at a Glance

  • FlashAttention-2 extends max context from 6,784 → 8,064 tokens (+18.9%) before OOM
  • INT4 KV-cache reduces per-token memory by 13.2% (0.183 → 0.159 MB/token)
  • Combined FA2 + KV-INT4 achieves lowest VRAM (2,090 MB) at max context (8,064 tokens)
  • Zero-shot quality preserved: ARC 62.5% | BoolQ 58.0% | HellaSwag 69.0%
  • Model: Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct at 4-bit NF4 = 1,917 MB weight footprint

🛠 System Architecture

Optimization Pipeline

📥 Input: HuggingFace Model + Prompt
↓
⚙ 4-bit NF4 Weight Quantization (bitsandbytes)
↓
💾 Model Loading with VRAM Budget (max_memory = 3.8 GiB)
↓
🔍 Attention Backend Selection (Eager / SDPA / FA2)
↓
🗜 KV-Cache Quantization (FP16 / INT4 / INT2)
↓
🚀 Autoregressive Generation (Greedy, batch=1, max_new=64)
↓
📊 Output: Latency, VRAM, Throughput, Correctness

Codebase Structure

LLMInference/
├ config.py                    # Central config: models, VRAM budget, benchmarks
├ src/
│  ├ model_loader.py          # 4-bit NF4 loading (bitsandbytes)
│  ├ attention_backends.py    # SDPA / FlashAttention-2 selection
│  ├ benchmark_harness.py     # Latency/throughput/VRAM measurement
│  ├ kv_cache_quant.py        # INT4/INT2 KV-cache (optimum-quanto)
│  ├ correctness.py           # Logit-trace token agreement
│  ├ quality_eval.py          # 0-shot ARC/BoolQ/HellaSwag
│  ├ profiler.py              # PyTorch Profiler kernel analysis
│  ├ vram_monitor.py          # GPU/CPU memory monitoring
│  └ utils.py                 # Logging, seeding, checkpoints
├ scripts/
│  ├ run_all.py               # Master orchestrator (weeks 03-10)
│  ├ generate_plots.py        # Matplotlib visualization
│  └ week03..week10_*.py      # Individual experiment scripts
├ prompts/fixed_prompts.json       # Reproducible test prompts
└ results/                         # All JSON results + plots

Key Code: 4-bit Model Loading

bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",          # NormalFloat 4-bit
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.float16,
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,     # Quantize the quantization constants
)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_id,
    quantization_config=bnb_config,
    device_map="auto",
    max_memory={0: "3.8GiB"},           # Hard VRAM cap
)

Result: Qwen2.5-3B compressed from ~6 GB → 1,917 MB (1.87 GB)

Key Code: KV-Cache Quantization

from transformers.cache_utils import QuantizedCache

kv_cache = QuantizedCache(
    backend="quanto",        # optimum-quanto backend
    config=model.config,
    nbits=4,                 # INT4 (or 2 for INT2)
)
output = model.generate(
    **inputs,
    past_key_values=kv_cache,  # Injected quantized cache
    max_new_tokens=64,
    do_sample=False,
)

🎯 Methodology

Experiment Timeline

WeekPhaseWhat Was Measured
03BaselineLatency, throughput, VRAM, OOM threshold (eager attention)
04ProfilingCUDA kernel breakdown, bottleneck identification

07KV-CacheINT4/INT2 prototype, memory-per-token measurement
08KV ExperimentsKV tradeoff analysis, zero-shot quality evaluation
09CombinedFA2 + KV-cache stability testing
10Full BenchmarkFinal consolidated results (6 configs × 3 lengths)

Evaluation Protocol

MetricHow Measured
p95 Latency95th percentile of 5 runs (2 warmup discarded), greedy decoding
Throughputoutput_tokens / mean_latency (tok/s)
Peak VRAMtorch.cuda.max_memory_allocated()
OOM ThresholdMax input length before crash, sweep Δ=256 tokens (128→8192)
CorrectnessTop-1 token agreement vs eager baseline (5 prompts × 20 steps)
QualityZero-shot log-likelihood on ARC, BoolQ, HellaSwag

🎯 Configuration Decision Guide

Use CaseBest ConfigWhy

💾 Lowest VRAMFA2 + KV INT22,086 MB at L=1024 — absolute minimum
🌐 Longest ContextFA2 + KV INT48,064 tokens + balanced latency
🔧 Zero-effort FallbackSDPA Default7,040 tokens — no code changes

🎓 Professor Cross-Questions & Answers

Category A: Fundamentals & Theory

Q1: Why NF4 over standard INT4?
NF4 places its 16 quantization bins at the quantiles of a standard normal distribution. Since neural network weights are normally distributed, each bin captures equal probability mass — minimizing quantization error. Standard INT4 uses uniform bins, wasting resolution on distribution tails.
Q2: What is double quantization?
Each block of 64 weights shares a FP32 scaling constant. Double quantization quantizes these constants to INT8, saving ~0.37 bits/parameter (~140 MB for a 3B model).
Q3: How does KV-cache scale mathematically?
M_KV = 2 × L × H × d × S × b_KV. For Qwen2.5-3B: 36 layers, 2 KV-heads, 128 dims. Measured at 0.183 MB/token in FP16. At 7,000 tokens: 0.183 × 7000 ≈ 1,281 MB — nearly exhausting the remaining ~2.1 GB after model weights.
Q4: How does FlashAttention-2 work?
It tiles Q, K, V into blocks fitting GPU SRAM (~100KB), computes partial softmax using the online softmax trick (running max + sum of exponentials), and combines results. The S×S attention matrix never exists in VRAM. Memory drops from O(S²) to O(S). Results are mathematically exact.
Q5: Why 87% token agreement for FA2?
Different FP accumulation order in tiled computation. FP16 is non-associative: (a+b)+c ≠ a+(b+c). Rounding differences propagate through softmax. The 13% disagreement involves tokens with nearly identical probabilities — semantically equivalent.
Q6: Why batch size = 1?
4 GB VRAM is the absolute edge case. Batch > 1 multiplies KV-cache cost. Batch=1 is the realistic single-user laptop scenario.

Category B: Methodology & Design

Q7: Why Qwen2.5-3B as primary model?
(1) 3B at NF4 = 1.9 GB, fits with ~2 GB headroom. (2) Uses GQA with 2 KV-heads — the modern paradigm. (3) Supports 32K context for wide OOM testing.
Q8: Why p95 over mean?
Mean is skewed by outliers. P95 = “worst case 95% of users see”. Standard production reliability metric.
Q9: How do you ensure fair comparison?
Fresh model load per config, reset_peak_memory_stats(), cuda.synchronize(), same prompts, seed=42.
Q10: Why fixed prompts?
Random text has unpredictable tokenization. Fixed prompts ensure identical token counts, attention patterns, and memory behavior. Eliminates confounding variables.

Category C: Results Analysis

Q11: Why didn't KV-INT4 alone extend OOM threshold?
At ~6,784 tokens, the bottleneck is the intermediate S×S attention matrix (hundreds of MB), not the KV-cache. Only FA2 eliminates this matrix, extending the threshold.
Q12: Why does KV latency overhead shrink at longer sequences?
Dequantization overhead is roughly constant. At short sequences it's a large fraction; at long sequences actual attention dominates. Classic amortization.
Q13: Why is FA2+INT4 faster than INT4 alone?
FA2 speedup on attention partially offsets dequantization overhead. FA2+INT4 (7,716ms) < Eager+INT4 (8,024ms).
Q14: BoolQ at 58% seems low?
4-bit quantized + zero-shot (no examples). Unquantized FP16 scores ~65-70%. The ~10-12pp drop is expected from NF4+INT4 compression. Few-shot would improve significantly.
Q15: Why no INT8 KV-cache?
optimum-quanto only supports INT2 and INT4. INT8 not implemented in v0.2.x.

Category D: Engineering

Q16: Why reload models between weeks?
CUDA memory fragmentation from thousands of variable-length inferences. Reloading resets the allocator.
Q17: What is CheckpointManager?
Saves progress after each week. If the 3-hour run crashes, resumes from last checkpoint instead of restarting.
Q18: How verify FA2 is active?
Check model.config._attn_implementation and look for flash_fwd_kernel in CUDA trace.

Category E: Extensions

Q19: Results on 8 GB GPU?
~6 GB headroom after weights. Baseline OOM at ~16K+ tokens. Relative improvements similar. Could test batch > 1 and 7B models.
Q20: 7B model on 4 GB?
7B at NF4 ≈ 3.5 GB weights, leaving ~500 MB for KV. OOM at ~2,700 tokens. FA2+INT4 extends modestly but severely limited.
Q21: Comparison to llama.cpp?
llama.cpp = CPU-based + optional GPU offload. Our approach = pure GPU = lower latency for short sequences. For long contexts, llama.cpp can use system RAM. Complementary.
Q22: Why not GPTQ?
GPTQ needs calibration data. NF4 via bitsandbytes: zero calibration, works with any HF model, supports double quant. GPTQ could improve quality 1-2%.
Q23: Speculative decoding?
Needs two models in VRAM simultaneously — impossible on 4 GB.

Category F: Reproducibility

Q24: How reproducible?
Very. Seed=42, greedy decoding, fixed prompts. Std dev = 9-38 ms on 6,200+ ms baselines. CV < 0.6%.
Q25: p95 over 5 runs valid?
p95 ≈ max with 5 samples. Acknowledged limitation. With deterministic decoding, variance is inherently minimal.
Q26: GQA architecture importance?
Qwen2.5-3B: 16 query heads, 2 KV-heads (8:1 ratio). KV-cache is 8× smaller than full MHA. Without GQA, OOM would occur much sooner.

Efficient Transformer Inference on Commodity Hardware

Jay Darji • Karma Patel • San Jose State University

Total experiment time: 9,894 seconds (~2h 45m) • RTX 3050 Ti (4 GB) • Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct
